title: Eliminating Flaky Tests in Playwright: Root Causes and Proven Fixes
excerpt: Flaky tests don't just waste CI minutes — they erode trust in your entire test suite. Here's how I diagnosed and fixed the most common sources of flakiness in Playwright across three production projects.
content:
*By Abdulkadir Akyurt*

Flaky tests are a morale problem as much as a technical one. Once engineers start dismissing red builds as "probably flaky," you've lost the safety net. I've watched teams reach that point, and getting them back to trusting their suite is harder than fixing the tests themselves.

Over the past two years, I've worked on three different projects running Playwright — versions 1.38 through 1.44 — and flakiness showed up in every one of them. The causes were never random. Once I started treating flaky tests as bugs rather than nuisances, patterns emerged quickly. This article covers the four root causes I encounter most often and the specific fixes that actually held up in production CI.

## The Waiting Problem Is Still the Biggest One

![](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1461749280684-dccba630e2f6?w=800&q=80)


Everyone knows not to use `page.waitForTimeout()`. The problem is that removing hard waits doesn't automatically make your tests stable — it just removes the most obvious smell. The real issue is implicit assumptions about application state.

Playwright's auto-waiting is genuinely good. When you call `page.click()`, it waits for the element to be visible, enabled, and stable before interacting. But "stable" means the element isn't animating. It doesn't mean the data behind it has loaded, or that a network request triggered by a previous action has completed.

The pattern I see constantly is something like this:

```typescript
await page.click('[data-testid="submit-button"]');
await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="result-table"]')).toBeVisible();
```

This looks fine. The table becomes visible. But the first row of data populates 300ms later from a separate API call, and the test that reads `row[0]` fails intermittently. The fix isn't to wait for the table — it's to wait for the data:

```typescript
await page.click('[data-testid="submit-button"]');
await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="result-table"] tbody tr')).toHaveCount(1, { timeout: 8000 });
```

Better yet, intercept the network request:

```typescript
const responsePromise = page.waitForResponse(
  resp => resp.url().includes('/api/results') && resp.status() === 200
);
await page.click('[data-testid="submit-button"]');
await responsePromise;
await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="result-table"] tbody tr')).toHaveCount(1);
```

The second approach ties your assertion to an actual application event, not to an arbitrary timeout. I've found `waitForResponse` to be one of the most underused tools in Playwright's API — it eliminates an entire class of race conditions.

One more thing on timeouts: Playwright 1.40 introduced per-assertion timeouts distinct from the global `timeout` setting. If your `expect` assertions are hitting the global 30-second timeout before failing, you have no idea how long individual assertions are actually taking. Set `actionTimeout` and `navigationTimeout` separately in your `playwright.config.ts` and you'll get much more useful failure data.

## Selector Brittleness Under Parallel Execution

Selectors that work perfectly in serial runs fall apart under parallelism, and the reason is usually shared DOM structure rather than anything wrong with Playwright itself.

Here's a concrete example. We had a modal component that our app rendered into a single `<div id="modal-root">` at the document root. In serial execution, one test opened a modal, asserted against it, closed it. Clean. Under parallel execution with multiple workers sharing the same browser context (a mistake we'd made in our config), two tests could both render into `#modal-root` at overlapping times. Selectors targeting `.modal-root .confirm-button` were matching the wrong modal.

The fix had two parts. First, we isolated each test into its own browser context using `test.use({ contextOptions: {} })` at the fixture level — this is standard advice, but it's worth confirming you're actually doing it rather than assuming. Second, we switched to more scoped selectors that anchored to specific dialog `aria-label` attributes:

```typescript
const dialog = page.getByRole('dialog', { name: 'Confirm deletion' });
await dialog.getByRole('button', { name: 'Confirm' }).click();
```

This also made tests more resilient to DOM restructuring, which is a bonus.

On the topic of parallel execution: Playwright's `fullyParallel: true` mode in version 1.39+ runs tests within a single file in parallel, not just across files. If your tests within a file share any state — a database record, a user session, a static test fixture — this will cause non-deterministic failures that look completely unrelated to the actual conflict. Audit your fixtures before enabling `fullyParallel`.

## Environment-Specific Flakiness in CI

![](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1537432376769-00f5c2f4c8d2?w=800&q=80)


The most frustrating flakiness category is tests that pass locally 100% of the time and fail in CI intermittently. I spent an embarrassing amount of time on one project before I understood what was happening.

The root cause in our case was font rendering. We were using visual regression testing with `expect(page).toHaveScreenshot()`, and our GitHub Actions runners were using a different set of system fonts than our developer machines. Screenshots differed by a few pixels in text rendering. The solution Playwright recommends is to use Docker-based runners with a fixed environment — the `mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.44.0-jammy` image exists precisely for this reason.

Beyond visual tests, CI flakiness often comes from resource constraints. GitHub Actions' `ubuntu-latest` runners are not generous with CPU. Tests that rely on animations completing within a specific time window will be slower on CI than on your M2 MacBook. I've started setting `slowMo: 0` explicitly (to confirm it's not set) and increasing animation-sensitive timeouts specifically for CI using environment variables:

```typescript
actionTimeout: process.env.CI ? 15000 : 8000,
```

This isn't ideal — ideally your app disables animations in test mode — but it's a practical fix when you don't control the frontend build.

Another CI-specific issue: Playwright's trace viewer and video recording both consume meaningful I/O on slower runners. Enable `trace: 'on-first-retry'` rather than `trace: 'on'`. You still get the trace for failures, but you're not writing multi-megabyte trace files for every passing test run.

## Test Isolation and State Leakage

This is the category that bites teams who've done everything else right. State leakage happens when a test leaves the application in a modified state that a subsequent test doesn't expect.

The most common form I see is leftover database records. Test A creates a user with the email `test@example.com` as part of its setup, then fails partway through without cleaning up. Test B, which also tries to create that user, gets a duplicate key error from the API and fails with a confusing message that has nothing to do with what it's actually testing.

The right approach is API-level teardown in `afterEach`, not `afterAll`. `afterAll` doesn't run if the test runner crashes mid-suite:

```typescript
test.afterEach(async ({ request }) => {
  await request.delete(`/api/users/${testUserId}`);
});
```

But teardown alone isn't sufficient. I also recommend generating unique test data per test run using a combination of `Date.now()` and a short UUID. Libraries like `@faker-js/faker` are useful here, but you don't need them for identifiers — a simple `test-user-${Date.now()}` keeps test data namespaced without dependencies.

For tests against a real database, consider transaction-based isolation if your stack supports it: wrap each test in a transaction and roll it back in teardown. This is faster than DELETE operations and guarantees a clean state regardless of what the test did. We implemented this on a Node/PostgreSQL stack using a custom Playwright fixture that injected a transaction-scoped database connection into each test.

## What Actually Moves the Needle

Flakiness rarely has a single cause. What I've found is that fixing the obvious issues — hard waits, bad selectors — reduces your flaky rate significantly but doesn't eliminate it. The remaining 20% is almost always state leakage or environment inconsistency.

The most useful diagnostic tool is Playwright's `--repeat-each` flag. Running `npx playwright test --repeat-each=5` on a suspected flaky test tells you within minutes whether it's genuinely non-deterministic. Pair that with `--trace on` and you have a complete record of what happened on each run.

If I had to prioritize: fix your waiting strategy first (it has the highest yield), then lock down your CI environment with the official Playwright Docker image, then audit your test data lifecycle. In my experience, teams that do those three things consistently get flaky test rates below 1% — which is the threshold where engineers stop dismissing failures and start trusting the suite again.

That trust is the actual goal. The tests are only useful if people believe them.
